articles.author: Bridget Brown, Bernard McGhee
articles.description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth will face a second day of grilling from Democrats on Capitol Hill, with senators getting their first opportunity to confront or praise him over the Iran war.
articles.title: Live updates: Hegseth faces another day of grilling on Capitol Hill over the Iran war

articles.author: Sara Cline, Jack Brook, David A. Lieb
articles.description: Louisiana’s top elected officials say the state’s congressional primaries won’t be going forward as scheduled in May as a result of a U.S. Supreme Court ruling. The high court on Wednesday struck down a majority Black congressional district. Republican Gov. Jeff Landry and Attorney General Liz Murrill say that means the state is prohibited from holding elections under the state’s current U.S. House districts. Early voting had been scheduled to begin Saturday in advance of the May 16 primaries. Landry and Murrill said they will work with the Legislature and state election officials to develop a plan.
articles.title: Louisiana congressional primaries suspended as a result of Supreme Court ruling, state officials say

articles.author: Christopher Rugaber
articles.description: President Trump’s pick to lead the Federal Reserve, Kevin Warsh, has said he wants to bring “regime change” to the central bank, but if confirmed by the Senate he will find a Fed already transformed by the White House’s attacks. For the first in almost five decades, there will be a former chair on the central bank’s board, potentially creating an alternate center of power. And on Wednesday multiple officials dissented from the Fed’s statement, a sign they won’t easily roll over for a new chair who has sharply criticized recent policy. It’s all a sharp contrast to the previous three Fed chairs — Ben Bernanke, Janet Yellen, and Jerome Powell — who were all Fed governors before becoming chairs in relatively smooth transitions.
articles.title: Unorthodox leadership change at the Fed: Warsh on deck while Powell remains

articles.author: Joey Cappelletti
articles.description: Congress gave approval to a short-term extension of a key U.S. surveillance program as they continue to work toward a long-term deal. The House had approved a three-year extension with slight reforms on Wednesday but attached legislation banning a central bank digital currency to win more votes. Senate leaders made clear that would not pass the chamber and instead opted for a short-term extension ahead of Friday’s expiration of the key provision of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act. The provision allows agencies like the CIA, National Security Agency and FBI to collect communications from foreign targets without a warrant, but can can incidentally sweep up Americans’ communications.
articles.title: Congress approves short-term extension of divisive US surveillance program

articles.author: Ben Finley, Stephen Groves, Meg Kinnard
articles.description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth clashed with Democratic lawmakers in Congress for a second day. At the start of a hearing Thursday in front of the Senate Armed Services Committee, Hegseth rejected senators’ accusations that the Iran war was launched without evidence of an imminent threat and waged with no coherent strategy. He called Democratic lawmakers “reckless naysayers” and “defeatists from the cheap seats.” He said they have failed to recognize the many successes of the military against the Islamic Republic over the last two months and in other operations since President Donald Trump returned to office.
articles.title: Hegseth clashes for a second day with Democratic lawmakers

articles.author: Jon Gambrell
articles.description: Iran's supreme leader says the Islamic Republic will protect its nuclear and missile capabilities as a national asset. Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei made the statement Thursday, likely drawing a hard line as U.S. President Donald Trump seeks a wider deal. Khamenei insists Americans belong “at the bottom” of the Persian Gulf. His remarks come as Iran's oil industry faces a U.S. Navy blockade. World oil prices surged above $126 a barrel. The U.S. and Iran are in a standoff over the Strait of Hormuz, impacting oil and gas exports. Trump floated new plan Thursday aimed at creating a maritime alliance to reopen the strait.
articles.title: Iran's leader vows to protect nuclear and missile capabilities

articles.author: Paul Wiseman, Christopher Rugaber
articles.description: Americans are paying for the war in Iran with every visit to the gas station, but some of the damage to the U.S. economy is being offset — for now anyway — by big tax refunds and an investment boom driven by artificial intelligence. According to a slew of economic data released Thursday, prices rose at the fastest pace in almost three years last month, U.S. economic growth is steady and layoffs fell last week. But an inflation gauge closely monitored by the Fed had its biggest gain in three years, driven by soaring gasoline prices.
articles.title: Tax refunds and AI boom have offset some US economic pain from Iran war and high gas prices, so far

articles.author: Ali Swenson, Meg Kinnard
articles.description: President Donald Trump says he's nominating Fox News Channel contributor Dr. Nicole Saphier for surgeon general after Casey Means’ path forward stalled in the Senate over questions about her experience and her stance on vaccines. In a social media post Thursday, the Republican president said Saphier is “a STAR physician who has spent her career guiding women facing breast cancer through their diagnosis and treatment.” Senators of both major political parties grilled Means on her vaccine stance and other health topics during a tense confirmation hearing, deepening doubts about her ability to secure the votes she needs for the position.
articles.title: Trump pulls nomination for surgeon general nominee Casey Means

articles.author: Michael Kunzelman, Alanna Durkin Richer
articles.description: Federal prosecutors have released a video showing the moment authorities say an armed man tried to storm the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner and attempt to kill President Donald Trump. Jeanine Pirro, the top federal prosecutor for Washington, posted the video on social media late Thursday. She says it shows Cole Tomas Allen shooting a U.S. Secret Service officer as he runs through security toward the gala packed with journalists, administration officials and others. Pirro says there is no evidence that the agent was hit by friendly fire. Allen agreed earlier Thursday to remain jailed while he awaits trial. He did not enter a plea during his brief appearance in federal court.
articles.title: Prosecutors release video of man storming correspondents’ dinner

articles.author: Collin Binkley
articles.description: An order coming from President Donald Trump calls for a new government website where people in the United States can find and compare private-sector retirement savings accounts. The order is intended to help more people gain access to retirement plans before next year. That's when the federal government will start matching retirement contributions made by low- and middle-income workers. Word of the executive order expected later Thursday comes from a White House official who spoke on condition of anonymity to discuss the order before it is formally announced.
articles.title: A new Trump order aims to give more people access retirement savings plans
